original_abstract: Abstract Transition metal oxides often undergo dynamic surface reconstruction under oxygen evolution reaction conditions to form the active state, which differs in response to the electrolyte pH. The resulting pH dependency of catalytic activity is commonly observed but poorly understood. Herein we track Co oxidation state changes at different pH-directed (hydr)oxide/electrolyte interfaces using operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy characterizations. Combined with in situ electrochemical analyses, we establish correlations between Co redox dynamics, the flat band potential and Co oxidation state changes to explain the pH dependency of the oxygen evolution activity. Alkaline environments provide a low flat band potential that yields a low-potential Co redox transformation, which favours surface reconstruction. Neutral and acidic environments afford an anodic shift of the Co redox transformation that increases the catalytic overpotential. The larger overpotential in neutral environments is attributable to poor Co atom polarizability and slow Co oxidation state changes. These findings reveal that interfacial Co oxidation state changes directly determine the pH dependency of the oxygen evolution reaction activity.

Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract AbstractTransition metal oxides often undergo dynamic surface reconstruction to an active state under oxygen evolution reaction (OER) conditions, which is pH-dependent. The pH dependency of the OER catalytic activity is commonly reported but not yet fully understood. In this study, the Co oxidation state at pH-dependent (hydr)oxide/electrolyte interfaces was probed via operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy. Based on the combination of in situ electrochemical measurements and X-ray absorption spectroscopy, the Co redox dynamics and the flat band potential were correlated with Co oxidation state changes to understand the pH-dependent OER activity. The low flat band potential at the alkaline condition resulted in low Co redox potential under the OER condition, which is favorable for the surface reconstruction. In contrast, the anodic shift of the Co redox potential at neutral and acidic conditions leads to the increase of the OER overpotential. The larger overpotential at neutral condition was ascribed to the lower Co atom polarizability and the slower Co oxidation state changes. The work here reveals that the interfacial Co oxidation state changes are the key factor to determine the pH-dependent OER activity.
